item.title: DHS HSIN Breach, Microsoft 365 Password Spraying, and Critical Adobe/Cursor Flaws
item.content:encoded:
Hackers target Microsoft 365 accounts with 81 million login attempts — Must Read

A large password-spraying campaign hit Microsoft 365 tenants with over 81 million login attempts, compromising 78 accounts across 64 organizations. Attackers abused Azure CLI and the ROPC OAuth flow to bypass MFA where Conditional Access policies were misconfigured or incomplete.

  • An aggressive password-spraying campaign targeting Microsoft 365 environments generated more than 81 million login attempts over a two-week period.
  • The threat actor attempted authentication through Microsoft's Azure command-line interface using still-valid username and password combinations exposed in past breaches.
  • Huntress observed the campaign between June 12 and 26 and confirmed that 78 Microsoft accounts were compromised across 64 organizations.
  • Huntress said many affected businesses had multi-factor authentication configured through Conditional Access policies, but those policies did not cover the ROPC authentication flow used by the attackers.
  • Huntress reported that the activity originated from an IPv6 range owned by LSHIY LLC and said it had not received a response after reporting the findings through the company's abuse portal.

Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic — Must Read

Adobe released urgent patches for seven maximum-severity CVSS 10.0 flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic that could allow arbitrary code execution. The company says no exploitation has been observed, but affected ColdFusion and on-premise Campaign Classic users should update immediately.

  • Adobe released patches for multiple maximum-severity security flaws affecting Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic.
  • The ColdFusion vulnerabilities could lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, arbitrary file system read, and security feature bypass, and they were fixed in ColdFusion 2023 Update 21 and ColdFusion 2025 Update 10.
  • Adobe Campaign Classic flaw CVE-2026-48286 has a CVSS score of 10.0 and could allow arbitrary code execution on affected systems due to incorrect authorization.
  • CVE-2026-48286 affects on-premise Adobe Campaign instances, including fully on-premise deployments and on-premise components in hybrid deployments, while Adobe-hosted instances have already been updated.
  • Adobe said it has not found any in-the-wild exploits for the issues and will move to twice-monthly security bulletins starting July 14, 2026.

Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands — Must Read

Two critical Cursor flaws dubbed DuneSlide could let a hidden prompt injection escape the editor’s sandbox and run arbitrary commands on a developer’s machine without user approval. The bugs affect all versions before Cursor 3.0, so users should update immediately.

  • Cato AI Labs found two critical flaws in Cursor, named DuneSlide and tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, that could let an ordinary-looking prompt escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s computer.
  • Both vulnerabilities affect every Cursor version before 3.0, and Cursor patched them in version 3.0 released on April 2.
  • The attack can be triggered through hidden instructions in content the Cursor agent reads, such as a connected service through the Model Context Protocol or a page returned by a web search, without requiring a user click or approval.
  • CVE-2026-50548 abuses Cursor’s handling of the run_terminal_cmd working_directory parameter to allow writes outside the intended project area, including to the macOS sandbox helper file.
  • CVE-2026-50549 abuses a faulty symlink safety check that can fall back to trusting an in-project shortcut path and write through it to a target outside the project.

DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform — Should Read

DHS confirmed hackers breached an unclassified Homeland Security Information Network environment used for sensitive information sharing among government and private-sector partners. The agency says it isolated affected systems, mitigated the vulnerability, and found no indication classified networks were impacted, while the scope of any stolen data remains unclear.

  • The Department of Homeland Security is investigating a cyberattack that compromised the Homeland Security Information Network, an information-sharing platform used by federal, state, local, international, and private-sector partners.
  • The intrusion was reportedly carried out by an unknown threat actor in recent weeks and is believed to have occurred sometime between late May and early June.
  • DHS said the incident involved a specific, unclassified legacy information-sharing environment and that there is no indication classified networks were impacted.
  • According to Nextgov’s sources, the attackers targeted HSIN servers as well as a SharePoint system used for collaboration efforts.
  • It remains unclear whether any documents were stolen from the system, and DHS has not attributed the attack to any specific threat actor or foreign government.

Ousaban Banking Trojan Targets Iberian Bank Users with Fake PDF Lures — Should Read

The Ousaban banking trojan is targeting Windows users in Spain and Portugal with fake corrupted PDF lures that geofence victims, hide the payload in an image, and rotate command servers daily. Once installed, it monitors banking sessions to steal credentials and enable account takeover.

  • Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs identified a May 2026 Ousaban campaign targeting Windows users who bank in Spain and Portugal.
  • The campaign starts with a phishing PDF disguised as a corrupted file that prompts the victim to press an “Atualizar” button, which opens a malicious webpage.
  • The malicious page screens visitors and shows an access denied notice to users outside Spain or Portugal instead of delivering malware.
  • After passing the check, a script downloads an image that looks like a PDF icon but hides a ZIP file containing Ousaban, then unpacks and runs the trojan.
  • Once running, Ousaban waits for users to open targeted banking sites and can capture screenshots and keystrokes, tamper with the clipboard, show fake messages, and give attackers remote control.

Healthcare’s Governance Needs To Speed Up To Combat Unsanctioned AI Usage — Optional

Healthcare organizations are facing growing risks from unsanctioned “shadow AI” use as employees adopt tools faster than governance and security controls can keep up. The key takeaway is that hospitals need faster, practical AI governance to close the gap between formal policy and real-world AI usage.

  • The article argues that healthcare governance needs to speed up to address unsanctioned AI usage.
  • Hospitals have spent the last decade modernizing their digital infrastructure.
  • Formal adoption of new tools in hospitals has been difficult because of technical and regulatory hurdles and sometimes resistance from employees.
  • Errol Weiss of Health-ISAC says there is a widening gap between how health sector organizations want to use AI and how AI is actually being used inside and outside their walls.
  • The article identifies shadow AI, governance, and closing the gap as major themes.

What Is Agentic Runtime Security? — Optional

Mitiga argues that traditional EDR is no longer enough because modern attacks move through cloud, SaaS, identity, AI agents, and third-party services where endpoint tools have little visibility. Its Agentic Runtime Security offering is positioned as log-based runtime detection and response for stopping active attacks across that broader infrastructure before business impact.

  • Mitiga describes Agentic Runtime Security as runtime detection and response across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services that anticipates, detects, interrupts, and stops active attacks before they reach the business.
  • The article argues that EDR protects endpoints and workloads, while Agentic Runtime Security uses platform audit logs to protect areas where endpoint agents cannot operate.
  • Mitiga says modern attackers move quickly across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services, citing figures such as a 22-second median handoff time for initial access in 2025 and a 29-minute average eCrime breakout time.
  • The article states that Mitiga’s approach depends on a forensic-grade, long-horizon data layer because cloud and service environments do not provide disk images or memory dumps like endpoints do.
  • Mitiga positions its Helios AIDR model around three pillars: Defend with AI, Defend from AI, and Defend the AI, all built on an AI-native foundation.

Webinar: Why traditional email security is no longer enough — Optional

BleepingComputer is hosting a July 8 webinar on how modern phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email security by abusing trusted identities and workflows. The session will focus on using behavioral AI to automate detection, investigation, and remediation.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 at 2 PM ET titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will be presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • The webinar will examine how modern phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • The article says many current email attacks impersonate trusted colleagues, vendors, and business partners instead of relying on malicious attachments, known malware, or suspicious domains.
  • The webinar will cover how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows for email security teams.

Turning Indicators into Intelligence in OpenCTI with Criminal IP — Optional

Criminal IP’s OpenCTI integration enriches IPs, domains, and URLs with reputation scores, infrastructure context, vulnerability data, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis. The goal is to turn isolated indicators into connected intelligence inside OpenCTI for faster triage, investigation, and prioritization.

  • The Criminal IP integration with OpenCTI enriches IP addresses, domains, and URLs with reputation scoring, infrastructure intelligence, vulnerability data, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis.
  • Criminal IP provides dual-perspective risk scoring for IP addresses, reflecting both inbound targeting and outbound behavior.
  • The integration structures enriched data as OpenCTI entities and relationships, including CVEs, Autonomous Systems, geolocation, and resolved IP infrastructure.
  • For domains, Criminal IP performs full URL analysis to detect phishing activity, credential harvesting, suspicious files, and impersonation techniques.
  • The article identifies SOC triage, threat hunting, infrastructure pivoting, and phishing campaign analysis as key use cases for the integration.

item.title: Oracle EBS CVE-2026-46817 Attacks, ARToken Phishing, and Japan Breach Disclosures
Tomcat CVE-2026-55957, Adobe ColdFusion RCE Patches, and AI Hallucinated Domain Abuse

Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 Access and Rolls Out Claude Sonnet 5
CitrixBleed CVE-2026-8451, BioShocking AI Browser Abuse, and PyPI Telegram Bot Backdoors
Langflow CVE-2026-33017, Malicious AI Extensions, and GuardFall Agent Risks
